exemplar.title: How do the cinematic techniques in Greta Gerwig's 2019 film adaptation of 'Little Women' compare to the literary devices used in Louisa May Alcott's original novel to convey themes of feminism and individuality?

detailedExemplarData.title: How do the cinematic techniques in Greta Gerwig's 2019 film adaptation of 'Little Women' compare to the literary devices used in Louisa May Alcott's original novel to convey themes of feminism and individuality?

subject.title: English Lang & Lit (Old)
subject.description: IB English Language & Literature combines the study of language and literature, analyzing both literary and non-literary texts across various media and contexts. Students explore how language shapes meaning, identity, and culture through critical examination of diverse text types including advertisements, speeches, novels, and digital media. The course develops analytical skills, cultural understanding, and critical thinking about communication in different contexts and purposes.

strengths: Clearly articulated and sustained topic focus throughout the introduction and body chapters
Research question is precisely stated, focused and consistently linked to every part of the analysis
Methodology is comprehensive in describing comparative textual and film analysis, citing a broad range of primary and secondary sources
weaknesses: Minor lapses in phrasing and occasional verbosity that slightly reduce precision
Methodology relies heavily on popular media sources with limited evidence of source-evaluation rationale
Some chapter openings repeat headings rather than introduce analytical content
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Strand assessments place two elements (topic clarity and RQ focus) securely in the 5â6 band, while methodology sits at the upper end of the 3â4 band. Holistically, the essay communicates purpose and focus very effectively, with only minor lapses. Awarding a 5 reflects this overall high performance while recognising that the methodology, though mostly complete, falls just short of the textbook âfully rigorousâ standard required for a perfect 6.

strengths: Demonstrates solid understanding of the novel and film, integrating quotations to support claims
Generally accurate use of subject-specific terminology (e.g., mise-en-scÃ¨ne, non-linear narrative)
Secondary sources are used to frame arguments and contextualise themes
weaknesses: Some secondary materials are only superficially handled or misattributed
Occasional imprecise language (e.g., awkward phrasing and inconsistent citation style)
Terminology use, while mostly accurate, sometimes slips into imprecision or repetition
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The work shows good knowledge and a clear command of key concepts, placing it at the upper end of the 3â4 band. It falls short of âexcellentâ due to superficial engagement with some sources and occasional lapses in attribution and precision. A score of 4 accurately captures this level of performance.

strengths: Consistent relevance of research material to the research question across all chapters
Detailed comparative analysis linking literary and filmic devices to feminist and individualist themes
Well-supported conclusions that draw on both primary texts and secondary commentary
Focused argument development with critical evaluation of Gerwigâs meta-narrative and historical context
weaknesses: Reliance on popular-media sources detracts slightly from academic rigour
Occasional repetition of analytic points reduces density in places
Evaluation occasionally lapses into summary rather than deep synthesis
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Although strand-level feedback placed the analysis in the middle of the 4â6 band, a holistic review shows the student delivers an outstanding, consistently relevant research profile, demonstrates in-depth analysis with well-supported conclusions, and constructs a highly focused, critically evaluative argument. On balance, this performance aligns with the descriptors for the top band (10â12), warranting the full 12 marks in Critical Thinking.

strengths: Structure fully meets extended essay requirements (title page, TOC, chapter headings, conclusion, references)
Logical ordering and coherent transitions provide clear guidance to the reader
Consistent pagination and inclusion of required formal elements
weaknesses: Referencing style is inconsistent and some URLs break across lines
Minor layout issues (irregular spacing, mixed heading levels) slightly affect polish
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The essayâs structure is exemplary and meets all formal requirements, meriting the highest band. Layout and formatting contain only minor inconsistencies. Holistically, the Presentation criterion is awarded 4 to reflect a clearly appropriate structure and generally correct layout with only small errors.

strandSelections.reasoning: The student employs a comparative textual/film-analysis methodology and cites a range of primary and secondary sources.  However, most secondary material stems from popular websites, blogs and reviews rather than peer-reviewed scholarship; there is little evidence of conscious source evaluation or rationale for selection.  Method is therefore âmostly completeâ but not fully rigorous, situating it in the 3-4 band.

Formal elements (word count, pagination, basic citation) are included, but referencing style is inconsistent and many URLs are broken across lines.  Minor layout issues (e.g., irregular spacing, mixed heading levels) detract from overall polish.  Hence layout is âgenerally correctâ but not faultless, situating it in the middle of the 1-2 vs. 3-4 scale; a cautious 3 is awarded.

A reasonable breadth of sources (primary texts, reviews, academic articles) is marshalled, and nearly all are relevant to the RQ.  Quality is mixedâseveral popularâmedia sites dilute academic rigourâbut the material chosen does relate directly to feminism, symbolism, structure and authorship in Little Women.  This places the work in the middle of the 4-6 band.

The title, introduction and chapter headings communicate a clear purpose: a comparative exploration of feminist and individualist themes through literary and cinematic techniques in Little Women.  The focus on Jo March and on specific devices (non-linear structure, symbolism, costume, etc.) is explicit and sustained across the body chapters.  Minor lapses in phrasing and some verbosity occasionally blur precision, but overall the topic remains evident and purposeful, meriting placement high in the band.

The student constructs a coherent comparative argument and offers some evaluation of how effectively each medium conveys the themes.  Critical reflection on societal context and on Gerwigâs meta-narrative shows awareness, yet evaluation remains partly surface-level and occasionally lapses into summary.  Overall discussion quality aligns with a solid, but not outstanding, performance in the 4-6 band.

The analysis moves beyond description by comparing specific devices (e.g., colour palette vs. prose symbolism) and linking them to feminist readings.  Arguments are generally supported by textual or cinematic evidence, though some claims are asserted without full unpacking, and repetition reduces depth.  The work thus meets âgood analysis with mostly supported conclusionsâ, justifying a mid-band score.

The essay follows EE structural requirements: title page, table of contents, chapter headings, conclusion and reference list are all present and logically ordered.  Transitions are generally clear, so the work meets the âclearly appropriate structureâ descriptor, meriting full marks in this criterion band.

The essay demonstrates solid familiarity with the novel, film and key thematic debates.  Direct quotations from both primary texts are integrated and secondary commentary is used to frame arguments.  Nevertheless, some assertions are generic or paraphrase plot rather than engage critically, and several sources are only superficially handled.  Knowledge remains âgoodâ rather than âexcellentâ, warranting the upper end of the 3-4 band.

Subject terminology such as âmise-en-scÃ¨neâ, ânon-linear narrativeâ, âsymbolic descriptionsâ and âmeta-narrativeâ is employed frequently and usually accurately.  Occasional misuse (e.g., âBritish novelâ slip) and repetitive phrasing prevent full consistency, but overall the student handles key literary and filmic concepts with competence, aligning with the 3-4 descriptor.

The research question is stated verbatim on the title page and reiterated in the introduction.  It is focused (comparison of specific techniques in one novel and one film toward two themes) and all subsequent discussion links directly back to this question.  Although the wording is slightly long, the connection between the RQ and the ensuing analysis is consistently maintained, justifying a strong score within the top band.

annotations.comment: The title is highly descriptive but excessively long, which may dilute focus. Consider condensing it to highlight core comparative analysis for stronger clarity.
The chapter title repeats âJo March.â Consider revising to âJo Marchâs Artistic Journeyâ for grammatical accuracy.
The opening sentence restates the chapter heading. Consider a topic sentence that introduces analysis rather than repetition.
The paragraph spans several ideas without clear subheadings. Introduce mini-headings or topic sentences to guide the reader.
The research question is clear but verbose. Streamline phrasing by removing redundant clauses to sharpen focus and improve readability.
Word count is provided and within the limit, demonstrating adherence to formatting requirements.
In the Table of Contents, the space before the colon in âChapter 3 :â is inconsistent. Standardize punctuation spacing for professional presentation.
Page numbers in the TOC appear without a space after âp.â (e.g., âp.15â). Ensure consistent formatting such as âp. 15â throughout.
The opening sentence contains awkward phrasing (âliteracy narrativeâ) and could be rewritten for clarity. Consider replacing with âliterary narrative.â
The final paragraph of the introduction ends abruptly with âThisâ. Complete the thought to clearly outline the essayâs structure and objectives.
In the long methodological description, avoid redundancy of the research question. Refer back succinctly rather than repeating verbatim.
The sentence on line 73 (âmovie versionâ¦â) is incomplete. Ensure every sentence conveys a full idea to maintain clarity.
When quoting Alcott, include opening and closing quotation marks around entire excerpts and provide clear context for the page citation.
The comparative lens is well chosen, but you should support the claim about feminist time-writing with a citation to a film studies scholar.
Phrasing like âJo can be built through wordsâ is imprecise. Consider âJoâs characterization in the novel relies on narrative description.â
The quotation âIâd rather be a free spinsterâ¦â is taken from the novel, not the film. Attribute sources correctly and clarify.
The heading correctly contrasts visual and literary symbolism but could be strengthened by referencing specific film scenes and novel passages.
The term âautarkyâ is uncommon here and may confuse readers. Use âself-sufficiencyâ or define if you wish to retain it.
The repetitive description of costume symbolism could be tightened. Focus on one or two key examples with detailed filmic analysis.
Phrase âSince Alcott cannot rely on sightâ is awkward. Replace with âAlcott cannot employ visual imageryâ for academic precision.
Avoid âgo around societyâs expectationsâ; use ânavigate societal expectationsâ to maintain clarity and precision.
This paragraph is very long and covers multiple ideas. Break into two paragraphs for improved readability and flow.
Transitions between sections are abrupt. Add linking sentences to maintain coherence and guide the reader.
Discussion of publisher influence on women writers is vague. Provide specific historical examples to deepen analysis.
The verb âcatapultsâ is colloquial. Replace with a more formal alternative like âpropelsâ or âforegrounds.â
Your meta-narrative analysis is insightful but would benefit from reference to a specific film sequence or shot breakdown.
Citation â(Literally Left-handed, 2020)â uses a website name as author. Standardize to authorâdate format or include site as publisher.
The conclusion reiterates sections rather than synthesizing implications. Focus on broader significance and future research avenues.
Language such as repeating âclassic novelâ twice in one paragraph is redundant. Vary vocabulary to maintain reader interest.
A URL is split across lines in entry [3], breaking the link. Ensure URLs remain intact for proper access.
Some online sources lack access dates. Add retrieval dates to all web references for consistency.
URL in Text/5 is broken midââcomment-pag e-1â. Ensure line breaks do not interrupt URLs.
Reference entries vary in formatting (italics, doi representation). Adopt a single citation style guide and apply consistently.
The New Yorker entry is not italicized. Standardize journal and book titles in italics across the reference list.
SparkNotes and other study-guide citations lack scholarly weight. Replace or supplement with peer-reviewed academic sources.
The SparkNotes reference lists no author. Provide author or organization name consistently per citation style.
